text:
When the Supreme Court issued an emergency order this week that protected the congressional district of a New York Republican, it intervened in a voting rights dispute, pre-empting the state’s highest court before it had a chance to act.

Since President Trump returned to the White House, the Supreme Court’s conservative majority has shown a willingness to short-circuit lower-court proceedings with a slew of emergency rulings in federal cases. But this was a rare instance in which the justices leapfrogged the state courts, too — a sign, legal experts said, that despite intense scrutiny of how the Supreme Court has been using its emergency docket, the conservative justices appear to be expanding its use rather than constraining it.

Unlike the court’s traditional “merits” cases, which arrive after months or years of lower-court consideration, emergency requests are fast-tracked with limited briefing and almost always without oral argument. The emergency docket has exploded in recent years, particularly in the second Trump administration, with the filings accounting for a significant part of the justices’ workload. While the quick-turn orders are technically place holders, they can effectively settle significant issues while litigation plays out in the lower courts.

The order in the New York case was unsigned and included no reasoning, as is typical. The three liberal justices joined in a 13-page dissent to assert that the majority had interfered with New York court proceedings in an inappropriate and unorthodox manner that could have far-reaching consequences for expanding the justices’ power.

The majority was grabbing “newfound authority,” wrote Justice Sonia Sotomayor, warning that the action could put the Supreme Court at the center of deciding every election-law dispute, even as states seek to redraw voting maps before midterm elections in November. The move also usurped state power, she observed, a violation of principles of federalism that conservatives promote in other instances.

“If this court’s grasping reach extends even to a nonfinal decision of a state trial court,” Justice Sotomayor wrote, “then every decision from any court is now fair game.”

The New York order was issued Monday evening, when the court also released a second emergency ruling in which it blocked California policies that limit what public schools can tell parents about their children’s gender transition at school. In that case, the conservative majority pre-empted a federal appeals court. Without full briefing or oral argument, the majority seemed to expand the reach of a decision it issued last term to find the state’s rules likely to be unconstitutional.

Taken together, the two nighttime orders were a reminder of the majority’s willingness to flex its power through the emergency docket, making substantive — if technically temporary — decisions over the sharp objections of the liberal justices.

Stephen I. Vladeck, a Georgetown University law professor and the author of a book called “The Shadow Docket,” said the emergency orders this week made the court “look like what so many regularly accuse it of being: a font of partisan political power.” The justices, he added, had gotten into a “bad habit” of granting relief before litigants had gone through the process in the lower courts.

“The court is intervening because it (thinks it) can, and because, for whatever reason, it doesn’t want to wait — in these cases, anyway — for the ordinary processes that would bring these issues to the court in due course,” he wrote in his newsletter.

At issue in the California case were state rules that generally restrict public schools from informing parents about their child’s social transitioning unless the child has consented. In one instance cited in the case, parents learned of their middle-school child’s gender transitioning at school after the child attempted suicide and was hospitalized.

Justice Amy Coney Barrett said the majority was not being impatient but was compelled to act by the court’s precedents to ensure parents have critical information about their children’s mental health. A group of Christian teachers and parents challenged California’s policies.

Religious liberty groups hailed the outcome as a groundbreaking victory for parental rights and praised the court for not waiting on the lower courts. But proponents of L.G.B.T.Q. rights denounced the court for failing to fully explore the potential harms to students or even litigate the contested question of what California’s policy actually requires — issues that lower courts would normally fully dissect before the Supreme Court took action.

In dissent, Justice Elena Kagan suggested that the majority could turn out to be right on what she called “thorny” legal questions. But skipping ahead of the U.S. Court of Appeals for the Ninth Circuit, she said, was out of line with how the Supreme Court would have handled a similar case just a decade ago. She noted that the court could wait and consider any one of 40 other cases testing similar issues that were making their way through the legal system on its normal timelines.

“Our processes are, in short, the hallmark of judicial probity, and alike its guarantor,” she wrote. “There was no reason to abandon them here.”

In the New York case, the majority blocked an order from a state court trial judge who had concluded that a New York congressional map, approved in 2024, violated the state Constitution by diluting the power of Black and Latino voters. The congresswoman, Nicole Malliotakis, had asked the justices to block the ruling and to preserve her district, the only one in New York City held by a Republican.

Under federal law, the Supreme Court does not have jurisdiction to hear appeals from state courts until a state’s highest court has ruled and issued final “judgments or decrees.” In the past, when the justices have provided emergency relief from a state-court decision, the top state court had either denied the request first or failed to act promptly.

That was not the case in the New York matter, where Ms. Malliotakis simultaneously appealed to the state’s intermediate and highest appeals courts. The top court transferred the matter, saying it must first be reviewed by the intermediate court, which eventually denied the congresswoman’s request. Ordinarily, the case could then return to the state’s Court of Appeals, but her request to the Supreme Court was already pending by then.

Stephanie Barclay, a constitutional law scholar at Georgetown, noted that the justices had recently cleared the way for Texas and California to use new voting maps in November that had been adopted by their legislatures and challenged in court. She said she saw the majority as following a similar approach in New York, declaring “neither state nor federal courts can interfere with election maps too close to the election.”

More broadly, she pushed back on criticism of the court’s handling of the emergency docket.

“The thing that is novel here is the critique,” said Professor Barclay, who was a law clerk to Justice Neil M. Gorsuch. “It is not the practice.”

Indeed, Justice Samuel A. Alito Jr., writing separately for himself, said it was imperative for the court to act because Ms. Malliotakis had “nowhere else to turn.”

Had the justices not intervened, he wrote, the clock was likely to have run out before they could review the matter. That would have allowed the “use of an unconstitutional district in the November election and the election of a member of the House of Representatives whose entitlement to the office would be tainted.”

Justice Sotomayor countered that the court’s action was an “astonishing, unexplained step,” acting before the state’s top court “had a chance to weigh in.”

title: Trump says Oklahoma Sen. Markwayne Mullin will replace Kristi Noem as DHS secretary
authors: Dale Denwalt, Alex Gladden, Alexia Aston, Josh Kelly
description:
Noem also came under fire for moving to spend millions on ads and luxury jets for the Department of Homeland Security.
He must be confirmed by his colleagues in the U.S. Senate.
Stitt said in a statement he would be appointing "a strong, small government conservative voice to support President Trump and protect Oklahomans’ way of life."

text:
President Donald Trump has named U.S. Sen. Markwayne Mullin of Oklahoma as the nation's next secretary of Homeland Security, placing him in charge of Immigration and Customs Enforcement as the agency faces intense scrutiny.

He will replace Secretary Kristi Noem, who faced harsh questioning by members of Congress in an oversight hearing following the agency's actions in Minnesota, where officers shot and killed two U.S. citizens. Noem also came under fire for moving to spend millions on ads and luxury jets for the Department of Homeland Security.

In a post on the president's social media site Truth Social, Trump said Mullin will take over the role on March 31. He must be confirmed by his colleagues in the U.S. Senate.

"It’s an honor to be nominated,” Mullin told reporters after the Thursday, March 5 announcement. “We’ve still got the nomination process.”

Asked about whether he’s considering any reforms to DHS, he said he wants to “get the Department of Homeland Security working for the American people.”

The Republican from rural Westville has been a fixture on national news broadcasts as a strong ally for Trump, with appearances in support of military action in Iran to defending the immigration officer who shot and killed Renee Nicole Good in Minneapolis. Mullin also defended Trump's decision to send the National Guard to Los Angeles during protests against ICE.

Mullin, 48, won his first election in 2012 to represent eastern Oklahoma in the U.S. House of Representatives. In 2022, following the death of longtime U.S. Sen. Jim Inhofe, Mullin won a special election for the seat.

He would have stood for re-election in 2026.

Mullin was known for a pugnacious attitude in as a member of Congress and for his substantial wealth. His net worth is an estimated $66.33 million, according to investment research site Quiver Quantitative, which ranks him 23rd most wealthy among members of Congress and second most wealthy among Oklahoma's members of Congress behind Rep. Kevin Hern.

Mullin gained national notice in 2023 for a feuding with the chief of the Teamsters union during a Senate hearing. The verbal dispute led to name-calling and an invitation to fight.

Before Trump's State of the Union address in February, Mullin was caught on camera trying to grab a sign being held by Texas Rep. Al Green. The sign read: "Black people aren't apes," a reference to a video posted by Trump that presented former President Barack Obama and first lady Michelle Obama as apes.

Mullin's seatmate in the U.S. Senate, Oklahoma's Republican senior Sen. James Lankford, said Trump has long wanted Mullin in his administration and tried earlier on in his second term to bring him on board.

“The president’s been on him to join the Cabinet for quite a while, Lankford told reporters.

Once Mullin officially leaves the seat, Oklahoma Gov. Kevin Stitt will have 30 days to appoint someone to serve the remainder of the term, which ends on Jan. 3, 2027. State law also requires, if necessary, a special election. Due to the short amount of time before the November elections, however, it's unlikely that a special election will be needed.

Stitt said in a statement he would be appointing "a strong, small government conservative voice to support President Trump and protect Oklahomans’ way of life."

Oklahoma Republican Congressman Tom Cole, who leads the powerful House Appropriations Committee, congratulated Mullin on social media after the news broke.

"Markwayne has been an instrumental leader in Oklahoma, consistently pushing conservative policies that have benefited the American people," Cole said. "I have no doubt he will do the same as a part of the Trump Administration."

Pat McFerron, a longtime member of the Oklahoma political scene and pollster at CHS & Associates, said he believed Mullin would get the Department of Homeland Security “going in the right direction.”

“Absolutely, he’ll be successful,” McFerron told The Oklahoman shortly after the news was announced on March 5. “Markwayne is successful at whatever he decides to do in life.”

McFerron said it is not surprising that Trump appointed Mullin to the position.

“He has a close relationship with President Trump and will be able to enact the Trump agenda and do so successfully,” McFerron said.

In recent months, Mullin had vocally backed Noem and the Trump administration's efforts to ramp up immigration enforcement and

After an ICE officer shot and killed a Minneapolis woman in January, Mullin defended the agent saying, "At the same time, we don't know what she has in that vehicle," Mullin said. "We don't know if she has weapons. We don't know what the situation is, but she clearly pulled in front of the ICE agents to block their path."

During his time in Congress, Mullin also became known as an advocate for tribal nations. Mullin, a Cherokee Nation citizen, is the only Native American member of the U.S. Senate.

Chuck Hoskin Jr., the principal chief of the Tahlequah-based Cherokee Nation, described Mullin's nomination to lead the Department of Homeland Security as "deeply encouraging to have someone with a keen understanding of federal Indian policy, law and justice elevated to such a critical leadership role within a powerful federal agency."

"We are confident in Secretary Mullin's ability to lead with historic responsibility and look forward to the positive, unifying impact his unique perspective will bring to the nation," Hoskin said in a statement.

Mullin is a former professional MMA fighter and owns owns a plumbing business, a ranch near Westville and several other properties in Oklahoma. He and wife Christie have six children, Jayce, Jim, Andrew, Larra, and adopted twins Ivy and Lynette.

Contributing: Zachary Schermele of USA TODAY.

(This story was updated to include new information.)

title: House Judiciary Committee hears a series of immigration bills - SteveAhlquist.news
description:
The House Judiciary Committee considered a number of bills related to immigration enforcement and civil rights on Wednesday.
But honestly, the biggest difference is that there has never been, since 2003, a coordinated effort to interfere with federal law enforcement agencies while in the middle of executing law enforcement operations.
There were no issues like we saw in Minnesota because there were no violent interactions and attacks on law enforcement officers.
Federal law and local law enforcement agencies have done these things long before ICE was an agency.
“I, in my professional role, regularly offer Know Your Rights trainings to community members who feel threatened by immigration enforcement.
text:
The House Judiciary Committee considered a number of bills related to immigration enforcement and civil rights on Wednesday. Committeemembers heard testimony on legislation aimed at mitigating the tactics of Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) and protecting state and federal constitutional rights. You can watch the video here.

I’m going to consider the bills in the order they were introduced.

First up was House Resolution7077, condemning ICE’s tactics. Representative Jason Knight (Democrat, District 67, Barrington, Warren) explained his resolution:

“This is a resolution condemning the unconstitutional tactics by U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement in the State of Rhode Island and beyond. I’ve got to tell you, I bring this resolution with a heavy heart because I can’t believe that we’re seeing this in the United States of America. I’ve been concerned about ICE’s behavior ever since the second Trump administration took office and the dogs were let loose, so to speak. It’s been awful. It’s been terrible to watch.

“But then ICE came to Warren, RI, where I was with my other representative from Warren, June Speakman. We were at a Veterans’ Day event, and we saw ICE in action right there on our streets in Warren. It wasn’t as bad as Minnesota, but it had all the hallmarks. It had the unmarked car. It had the masked agents... They snatched up a guy off the street who was working at Blount Foods and sped off, leaving his car abandoned in the middle of the road, creating tremendous amounts of confusion in our community. Our police weren’t notified...

“Nobody was hurt, but it scared the hell out of the community. It prompted me to have this resolution drafted.

“We can’t tell the federal government what to do, but we can be witnesses. We can say as a community that we, as one of the 50 United States of America, deplore this kind of behavior on the part of our federal law enforcement agents. In fact, these guys aren’t even doing that job anymore. They are engaged, as we saw in Minnesota, in brazenly unconstitutional tactics, creating danger in the community and to the officers, sowing fear.

“And it’s not like they have to do this. ICE existed long before President Trump came around. They did their job, and they did it professionally. It’s not an easy job that they have, but this is not ICE doing their job anymore. This is President Trump co-opting this federal law enforcement agency to execute a profoundly racist policy of stopping any person who’s not white and saying, “Show us your papers,” backed up by Justice Kavanaugh, who said it’s okay.

“Rhode Island has a voice in this matter, and we should pass this resolution. As I said, it’s not an immigration thing, and I don’t bring it up to be an immigration debate. I prefer that we have a strong border, and I understand that no nation exists very long without one, but this is about what somebody is doing in our name to enforce federal immigration law, and it’s just plain wrong.”

Representative Knight’s assertions did not go unchallenged. Representative Michael Chippendale (Republican, District 40, Foster, Glocester, Coventry) defended ICE and blamed the public opposition to ICE’s tactics on “political hysteria.”

“I am respectful of your position and the passion behind this issue here for everyone. It’s clearly exceptionally emotional, and we all have strong feelings and passions about it. That being said, I want to preface this with a lesson that I learned from you, because you are an accomplished defense attorney and former prosecutor. When I was complaining about a gun bill, you told me, ‘Chip, nothing is unconstitutional until a judge says it is.’ So I take issue with the word ‘unconstitutional.’

“But more to the point, you are correct that ICE has been operating since 2003. They’ve been doing their job in immigration control, and there is a difference now. Unfortunately, it’s very easy and common for folks to say, ‘It’s the orange man who’s doing it because he’s bad,’ and we all want to hate on that. But honestly, the biggest difference is that there has never been, since 2003, a coordinated effort to interfere with federal law enforcement agencies while in the middle of executing law enforcement operations.

“I don’t know if it ever happened, but if it has, I’m sure it was very isolated and certainly not to the extent that we saw here. I think there are plenty of contrasts we can look at, including what we saw just up in our northern neighbor, Maine, where they had a similar operation to what was happening in Minnesota. But what the Mainers did, rather than interfere with law enforcement operations, was invoke their First Amendment right to petition the government and to assemble themselves peaceably and protest. And they did that. ICE went in, did their job, and left. There were no issues like we saw in Minnesota because there were no violent interactions and attacks on law enforcement officers.

“There’s a lot of language in this resolution about mixed-status families and the detention and children being separated, but this is not new. This happens every year, and you saw this when you were in the Attorney General’s office. You see this every day when we have narcotics and domestic violence arrests: families are separated regrettably.

“That happens in law enforcement actions. These are not new things. The repeated assertions and remarks about unmarked vehicles and masks, again, are not new. Federal law and local law enforcement agencies have done these things long before ICE was an agency.

“However, when there are concerted efforts to interfere with the operations of federal law enforcement officers, which include and have included threats on their lives that have increased something like 8,000%, actual physical violence on officers, doxing, and actual physical violence on their families, it is necessary, for their safety, for them to conceal their identities.

“There’s a lot, I think, being inferred by folks when they see these things because of that overarching desire to be opposed to this administration. And I understand that, but it doesn’t justify assigning these things as being unique to this president.

“Presidents Obama, Bush, and Biden all removed and detained people from this country through ICE. If this committee wants to have a sincere conversation about immigration policy, to the extent that we have any impact on it, we can do that... [but] I think for us to pass a resolution condemning federal law enforcement for doing their job, essentially based on political hysteria, is inappropriate.”

Representative Leonela Felix (Democrat, District 61, Pawtucket) introduced House Bill 7211, the Rhode Island Community Protection Act. “It’s a transparency and public safety bill,” said Representative Felix. “It requires that all law enforcement officers, when performing routine duties and interacting with the public, do not wear face coverings and that they identify themselves.” Representative Felix introduced the bill because “we need to be able to hold law enforcement accountable for the work they’re doing… We need to build trust between law enforcement and our communities. Wearing masks and not identifying themselves … erodes public trust.”

Barrington Police Chief Colonel Michael Correia, speaking as the President of the Rhode Island Police Chiefs Association (RIPCA), opposed the anti-masking bill as written, because it applies to all law enforcement officers, not just ICE.

“I don’t condone or support at all some of the shoddy police work, investigative work that’s been done by ICE,” said Colonel Correia. “Our opposition is that we would rather have bad actions condemned and not the masks. What I don’t support is the innuendo that there are issues in Rhode Island Policing… so we don’t support the bill as it’s written, and we’re on the record saying don’t condone the shoddy police and investigative work of ICE.”

Kevin Love Hubbard, counsel for the Lawyers’ Committee for RI, spoke from experience:

“I represent clients on a pro bono basis in habeas corpus proceedings, and my clients describe an incredibly common experience. When they’re stopped by masked federal agents who are in tactical gear with no names and no badges, they can’t tell whether they’re being kidnapped or arrested. They cannot identify the person who siezes them. They have no way to seek accountability afterwards for violations of their constitutional rights. This is not business as usual for the federal government. This is new, and we need a response from state governments to correct the overreach. When a masked, unidentifiable agent seizes a person, that seizure is not an example of legitimate exercise of the people’s authority. It’s an exercise of raw power. A mask only serves one purpose: It can’t protect the officer. It can’t stop a bullet. It can’t deflect a blow. The only thing it does is hide that person’s identity and serves to eliminate accountability under the law. House Bill 7211 responds to this crisis of public safety and trust.”

Representative Leonela Felix also introduced House Bill 7198, colloquially referred to as the 364 bill. She explained:

“This bill modifies our sentencing laws by one day. That may sound like an insignificant thing to do in sentencing law, but the reason is that there is a loophole in federal immigration law that allows someone to be deported if the offense they have been charged with carries a potential sentence of one year or more.

“For example, if you are caught shoplifting, even if you had a filing and never stepped into jail, because the way our statute is written, you could have potentially served a year or more, and that qualifies as an offense for deportation. What this bill would do is reduce one year to 364 days, so that for simple nonviolent offenses, folks won’t get caught up in the deportation machine, protecting folks from deportation.

“I want to emphasize that this does not apply to crimes of violence, firearms, domestic violence offenses, or any serious offenses. This would be for offenses that are colloquially known as petty misdemeanors.”

“It’s a fundamental concept of justice that punishments should fit crimes and that more serious offenses should be punished more seriously than less egregious offenses,” said Attorney Carl Krueger, speaking on behalf of the ACLU of Rhode Island. “Not all crimes are equal. The proposed bill doesn’t reclassify any felonies as misdemeanors. It does not in any way change the punishment that might be imposed for felony convictions. What this bill does is reduce the maximum punishment that can be imposed on an individual convicted of a misdemeanor by one day. Why is this important? Because under the immigration laws, there is a body of crimes for which individuals can be deported. Being put in this category results from the crime being punished by a year or longer in jail, or from the potential punishment for that crime being a year or longer.

“Basically, the immigration law is straddling this one year on the wrong side of the line. It’s basically scooping up every single misdemeanor that can be punished for up to a year and putting it into this category of more serious crimes. What this bill would do is put the line back where it’s supposed to be.”

Representative Brandon Potter (Democrat, District 16, Cranston) introduced House Bill 7202, the Rhode Island Federal Constitution Defense Act. Representative Potter explained:

“The legislation before you creates a Rhode Island state cause of action, allowing Rhode Islanders to bring civil claims when and if a federal official acting under the color of federal law violates their rights under the U.S. Constitution. In other words, if someone’s First Amendment rights are violated or suppressed, if they are unlawfully detained in violation of the Fourth Amendment, if their general due process rights are ignored, if God forbid, we see a national tragedy here like we have seen in Minneapolis, Rhode Islanders would have the ability to seek relief through our state courts.

“I want to make two points of argument here. One, how this bill protects Rhode Islanders. First, it would obviously allow them to seek a remedy in state court if, unfortunately, their constitutional rights are violated. It would allow them to get some sense of justice. But more importantly, I would argue, it would hopefully create a deterrent effect. It would send a clear message and put federal actors on notice that they are not above the law when they operate in Rhode Island. And the violation of Rhode Islander’s constitutional rights will not be tolerated here.

“Qualified immunity and similar doctrines would not become shields for unconstitutional conduct. Now, I anticipate that some may have concerns about the constitutionality of this bill and about federal preemption or the Supremacy Clause, so I want to make a few things clear upfront. This bill does not interfere with the federal authority to enforce federal law. It does not conflict with any federal law because there is no federal statute that prohibits states from providing remedies for unconstitutional actions. And federal officials have never been authorized to violate a person’s constitutional rights in the first place.

“This legislation simply ensures that if and when someone’s constitutional rights are violated here in Rhode Island, there is a meaningful avenue for accountability. And lastly, this bill has been signed by 39 members of the House. We have a majority of support in the House on this bill.”

“Why do we need this legislation?” asked Attorney Jennifer Wood, director of the Rhode Island Center for Justice. “I, in my professional role, regularly offer Know Your Rights trainings to community members who feel threatened by immigration enforcement. I was recently doing a training for childcare workers with the SEIU, and never in my career have I received so many questions from U.S. citizens who are terrified of having their constitutional rights violated by ICE.”

Attorney Wood continued:

“I went through the whole presentation. I talked about the Fourth Amendment and the Fifth Amendment, and that these constitutional rights remain in place for U.S. citizens and all persons present in the United States. After I gave the whole spiel, the childcare workers turned to me and said, ‘Well, that’s very cute, Jen, but what do we do if an ICE officer kicks down the front door of the home in which we are providing childcare services?’ And I take a deep breath and gulp and say, ‘Well, then we’ll have to sue them for you afterwards,’ because constitutional rights are often vindicated after the fact.

“Without this legislation, we will be much more constrained in trying to bring these cases and struggling to provide accountability for those childcare workers who look me in the face and say, ‘Well, we love your speeches, but give us some proof that it’s going to stand up.’ We need this legislation to protect not just our immigrant community members, but all community members.”

Representative Carol Hagan McEntee (Democrat, District 33, Narragansett, South Kingstown), who chairs the House Judiciary Committee, introduced a similar bill, House Bill 7352, the Rhode Island Civil Rights Enforcement Act. Representative Hagan McEntee explained:

“It establishes a cause of action against state government entities for the violation of civil rights. This legislation does not create any new rights. It simply restores a [previously available] remedy for when a person’s rights under the Rhode Island or United States Constitution are violated. As the law currently stands, many constitutional rights exist without a meaningful way to enforce them. A right without a remedy is not truly a right. Twenty-three states have provided a civil remedy for violations of state constitutional rights. Rhode Island should do the same. Passing this bill will not create a flood of lawsuits. Rhode Islanders previously had this right of action, and there was no surge of litigation.”

Representative José Batista (Democrat, District 12, Providence) introduced House Bill No. 7360, the Protect Our Courts Act. Representative Batista explained:

“This bill is not, as much as I wish it were, an outright prohibition on federal agents, like ICE, to make arrests in or near our courts. It is a set of regulations that requires federal agents effectuating an arrest to do so only with a judicial warrant. If you’re going to arrest someone, there has to be a legal process. It can’t be something handed down or signed off on by a supervisor or bureaucrat; it has to be signed off on by a judge.

“We can’t skimp on the process just because the current holders of political office deemed this to be some made-up emergency.

“Why is this bill necessary? As an attorney and somebody who dedicates himself to serving middle and lower-class clients, I know that people are afraid to go to court, and that is a bad thing. This bill is pro-public safety. When there is a survivor of domestic violence who’s afraid to go near a court because they may be arrested, that makes us less safe. When a tenant can’t go to court to testify about habitual civil violations, we’re less safe. When witnesses don’t want to go near a courthouse, it makes it more difficult to convict people who do something wrong. So if you are for public safety, you should be for this bill.

“Regarding questions of legality or constitutionality, I am very pleased to say that this bill is modeled on legislation filed in New York State. The Trump Administration challenged the New York law, and that challenge failed.”

“I would like to note that before this administration, it had been the Immigration and Customs Enforcement’s written policy to not go into sensitive places, including courthouses, for the reasons that Representative Batista has stated,” noted Representative Felix. “That policy actually changed when this administration came into power, and that has created havoc within the courts... This wasn’t a law, but a policy in place for a long time, and it worked perfectly well, allowing folks to go through the legal process without the fear of immigration picking them up just because they went to court.”

Jenni Nguyen, Deportation Defense Organizer at the Providence Youth Student Movement (PrYSM), spoke about the impacts on Rhode Island’s Southeast Asian community members:

“Southeast Asian community members here in Providence, Cranston, Woonsocket, and throughout Rhode Island became refugees in the ‘70s and ‘80s after the Vietnam War and the secret, covert wars in Laos and Cambodia, and have subsequently been dropped into already underserved and heavily policed Black and brown neighborhoods here in the U.S. and in Rhode Island. Now, for over 30 years, many have been living in fear of ICE, some over wrongful convictions when they were just children and minor offenses. A good chunk of those who were detained at the Wyatt Detention Center, under horrible conditions, and have since been deported back to countries they were forced to leave as children under American intervention and bombings.

“As ICE expands its reign of terror and money-making schemes across the country, I support bills to ensure that our public facilities and courthouses in Rhode Island are not used and abused to further their agenda. Furthermore, as a Southeast Asian, I support House Bill 7198 to ensure our community members are not subject to unjust and harsh immigration consequences from simple misdemeanors that will drastically change their lives. We are especially interested in conversations surrounding the post-conviction relief. So if we want to continue that conversation, we are definitely open to that. And in conclusion, I urge you to carefully consider these bills for the good of all Rhode Islanders, and especially our Southeast Asian community who needs our legislators to act now before it is too late to protect our fathers, mothers, cousins, and brothers from unjust deportation, too many of whom have already been kidnapped by ICE and twice displaced for the color of their skin.”

Representative Joshua Giraldo (Democrat, District 56, Central Falls) introduced House Bill 7434, prohibiting ICE employees, agents, or officials from being within 200 feet of any polling place during voting. An amendment suggested by the Rhode Island Board of Elections would ensure that employees or ICE agents are not disenfranchised when entering a polling place solely to vote or return their own ballot. Representative Giraldo explained:

“Why is this necessary? Because words matter and rhetoric has consequences. As discussed, many national political figures, including advisors to the President, have publicly suggested having ICE agents stationed outside polling locations. When statements like that are made, particularly in the current climate, immigrant communities hear a message that is aimed at intimidation. I know this because my family immigrated here from Colombia, and I’ve lived my entire life in a community of immigrants.

“Like everyone in this room, I feel I have basic common sense and can understand when language is being coded. In communities like Central Falls, where many residents are either immigrants, first-generation, naturalized citizens, or live in mixed-status families, the presence of ICE vehicles or agents near a polling location will absolutely deter lawful voters from participating.

“I wish I didn’t have to put this bill in, but we simply don’t have the luxury of assuming that federal resources won’t be used in a way that is meant to intimidate people. We have seen real-life examples from across the country where armed ICE officials have abused their power without reprimand, and also examples of armed self-appointed election monitors being stationed near ballot drop locations or polling places, which, over the years, have thankfully led to DOJ investigations.

“Even if no direct enforcement action occurs, the intimidation effect is real. I want to take you through a hypothetical situation. Imagine a naturalized citizen whose spouse or parent is undocumented. They get out of work, they head down to their local school to go and vote and exert their constitutional right, and see a group of unmarked vehicles, masked ICE agents stationed in their neighborhood outside of the polling place, knowing that they would need to walk past them to go and cast their vote. From watching the news, we know that ICE’s power has become far less checked, and as of 2025, 40% of people held in ICE detention had no criminal convictions apart from immigration issues. Not to mention that ICE has also erroneously detained hundreds of American citizens, and when that has happened, it has not always provided the best explanation as to why it happened.

“I hope the committee can understand the real fear that people feel when they or their family could be randomly questioned, followed, scrutinized, and that this is serious enough to make them turn away [from their polling place]. That’s not how we should be functioning. Rhode Island has a history of protecting voters through laws on buffer zones and anti-intimidation. To me, this bill is a targeted preventative measure to ensure that federal immigration enforcement presence is not used intentionally or unintentionally as a tool of voter suppression.”

Secretary of State Gregg Amore (Democrat) testified in favor of this bill, noting that under the law, states control elections, not the federal government, adding, “Representative Giraldo said words matter. Words do matter. When the President of the United States talks about nationalizing elections, when one of his former advisors said, ‘We’re going to place ICE agents at the polling locations,’ that is unacceptable. It should be unacceptable to all Americans. Rhode Islanders and all Americans have the right to vote without intimidation, and that includes not being afraid of what awaits them at the polling place. The language the voter speaks, their appearance, or the color of their skin should not abridge the right to cast the ballot. But even more so, it should not be a pretext to question their legitimacy as Americans. That in and of itself is un-American.”

Representative Giraldo’s next bill has become a perennial, and no version has passed out of either chamber of the General Assembly. Upon passage, House Bill 7436 would prohibit state and municipal officials or bodies from entering into and/or renewing any agreements used to detain individuals for federal civil immigration violations. Representative Giraldo explained:

“I’ve come before this committee many times over the years, looking to pass this bill. The bill refers to entities like the Wyatt Detention Center, which is located in my district, pays no taxes, contracts with ICE, and takes asylum seekers from the border of Arizona to Rhode Island. The Wyatt is known for having a prisoner escape on New Year’s Eve a few years ago and the death of their inmates. This bill means they and others will not be able to enter into agreements that primarily serve to detain individuals for civil immigration reasons. It also seeks to phase out existing contracts, prohibit future spending, and transition us away from practices I feel aren’t aligned with Rhode Island’s values.

“Lastly, it prohibits the leasing of public land for use as an immigration detention facility, ensuring local control over our public assets. This will ensure that our public land and contracts continue to serve Rhode Islanders and are used for schools, public safety, housing, and recreation, not for federal civil detention.

“I’d like to take a moment to explain what the bill does not do. It does not prevent cooperation with federal law enforcement in criminal cases. It only applies to civil immigration matters. It does not stop law enforcement from working with federal agencies in criminal investigations. It does not mandate the release of individuals held on criminal charges. This is not about releasing people who pose a threat to public safety. It’s about preventing Rhode Island from acting as a contracted jailer for the federal government’s civil immigration system.

“It does not affect deportation proceedings. Federal immigration authorities can still enforce the law. This bill mandates that Rhode Island will not be part of detaining people in that process. This affirms our stance on treating every individual with dignity and respect, and it sends a strong message against the inhumane treatment of immigrants for civil violations.

“For Central Falls, the district I represent, this bill would affect Wyatt and transition it away from the model that has been a source of controversy and pain for our community for a long time. This change opens the door for more humane and just treatment of all individuals. As you all know, Rhode Island is not alone in its efforts in this. States across the nation, including many of our neighbors in New England, are reevaluating their stance on immigration detention and the use of public resources for such purposes. Many of you know that Providence has taken a stance regarding its public properties. By passing this bill, we join a growing movement that prioritizes human dignity, along with many other states like New Jersey, Illinois, California, and Oregon. I’m sure there are many more.”

Representative Karen Alzate (Democrat, District 60, Pawtucket, Central Falls) introduced House Bill 7439, the ICE Out Act, to “prohibit law enforcement agencies from hiring individuals who were hired as a sworn officer of the U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement Agency on or after January 20, 2025 and would apply prospectively to an individual hired on or after October 1.” Representative Alzate explained:

“This bill represents a thoughtful and proactive step towards strengthening public confidence in our state’s law enforcement recruitment practices. By establishing that law enforcement agencies may not employ individuals who were hired as sworn officers of the Immigration and Customs Enforcement Agency on or after January 20, 2025, and applying this prospectively beginning October 1, 2026, the legislation ensures clarity, consistency, and accountability in our public safety system. This bill is a common-sense measure that reflects Rhode Island’s values, fairness, transparency, and community trust. It recognizes the legitimate concerns many Rhode Islanders have regarding law enforcement practices that may undermine trust between community members and those sworn to protect them.

“By setting clear perimeters for hiring decisions, this bill supports law enforcement agencies in building stronger, more positive relationships with the diverse communities that they serve. Moreover, this bill does not affect current officers employed before the law’s effective date, ensuring respect for existing personnel while setting a forward-looking standard for future recruitment. In an era when public trust is in short supply and policing is essential to community safety and cohesion, this bill offers Rhode Islanders a balanced, responsible approach to promoting accountability, protecting civil liberties, and pursuing a justice system that works for all residents.”

Representative Alzate also introduced House Bill 7637, prohibiting the denial of an opportunity to apply for employment as a law enforcement officer or firefighter solely based on a person’s country of citizenship. Essentially, the bill, upon passage, allows a green card holder to become a police officer. The bill was created with the help of students attending William E. Tolman Senior High School in Pawtucket, RI. Representative Alzate explained:

“This bill comes to me from constituents, friends of mine, who have grown up in Rhode Island, their legal status is legal permanent resident, and they are interested in becoming police officers, but have been denied because they are not yet U.S. citizens. Rhode Island is home to a richly diverse population. Many communities across our state, from Providence to Patuckett, Central Falls, Woonsocket, Newport, and even Westerly, have neighbors who speak multiple languages, understand different cultures, and have lived here for years as lawful permanent residents. These residents are deeply invested in their neighborhoods and our state, yet current eligibility rules automatically exclude these committed community members from even applying to serve as police officers based on their legal and/or citizenship status, regardless of their qualifications. This bill would change that by ensuring that qualified lawful permanent residents seeking citizenship may apply to become police officers and complete their academy training.

“The bill maintains important protections: background checks, training requirements, and certification standards that would continue to apply as they do now, and individuals would become sworn officers only after becoming U.S. citizens, as required by current law. This benefits Rhode Island in several important ways: It builds trust in public safety, enhances public safety and cooperation, and strengthens recruitment and retention. As we know, law enforcement agencies across Rhode Island have been facing staffing challenges. Broadening the pool to include qualified lawful permanent residents expands the opportunity for strong candidates eager to serve their communities and helps meet staffing needs without lowering professional standards. This bill doesn’t weaken standards. It broadens opportunities while keeping professional requirements in place. This bill is a common-sense step towards a more inclusive, effective, and trusted public safety workforce in Rhode Island, and it also helps Rhode Islanders become U.S. citizens.”

Representative Chippendale, a Republican and House Minority Leader, supports the bill. He explained:

“This bill seeks to solve the myriad problems that we have right now. The staffing shortage is something we’ve been trying to address from every angle. I see this as inspiration for folks who are stuck in a tedious immigration process that needs federal action. This bill incentivizes folks to see the process through. I have often heard, certainly since 2010 in this building, people say, “We need people who look like us, whether it’s a teacher or a fireman,” and that couldn’t be truer. And it’s more than just looking like us. It’s about the wonderful cultural and ethnic diversity in our state being represented in public service positions such as police and fire. These are beneficial things. They do, as you point out, instill or rebuild trust among law enforcement, anyone in public service, and the community they serve.

“I’m a white Irish guy, so how can I compare that to what might be happening in Central Falls? We would run towards the police when we were kids if something went wrong. I understand today it’s not the same, and we want that back. This measure is a simple way to accomplish that. You’re really not moving a lot here, but the impact, were this to pass, would be multifaceted and tremendous.”

Representative Arthur Handy (Democrat, District 18, Cranston) introduced House Bill 7689, which would change the Rhode Island Works program, the state’s financial assistance and work support program for very low income families “to allow lawful permanent residents to receive benefits without a waiting period and increase the monthly cash benefit to one hundred percent (100%) of the federal poverty level.” Representative Handy explained that “it’s not just an immigration bill. It’s a workforce, job training, and child stability bill. Nearly 70% of the participants in the Rhode Island Works program are children. At its core, the bill is about helping to improve the lives of children.”

“This bill will get families, people, and community members the help that they need,” said David Veliz, executive director of the Olneyville Neighborhood Association. “From an anti-poverty perspective and a human perspective, this bill makes sense.”

Representative Jennifer Stewart (Democrat, District 59, Pawtucket introduced House Resolution 8121 to create “an 11-member task force to track and oversee immigration enforcement and support actions in Rhode Island.” Representative Stewart explained:

“The goal of the task force is to facilitate communication, understanding of intended and unintended consequences, and responsive problem-solving while building transparency, accountability, and trust; three things that have suffered since federal actions for immigration enforcement have intensified across the country, and in some cases, become quite aggressive and militarized. According to the U.S. Census Bureau American Community Survey, 2023 data, foreign-born residents comprise nearly 15% of our state’s population. Many of us have experienced immigration at some point in our family history. We have a very diverse population, and incidents like the one in which a high school intern working at the Rhode Island Superior Court was erroneously detained for questioning should remind all of us that you can’t tell someone’s status by the way they look.

“Moreover, the Trump administration has set a nationwide daily goal of taking into custody 3000 people a day and deporting a million per year. Across the country, there are backlogs in processing detainees, uncertainty about what happens to people taken into custody due to delays and other problematic conditions, and mistakes. Consequently, the wellbeing of immigrant Rhode Islanders touches all of us and should be a concern.

“I hope it is not controversial to create a task force like this. The State of Rhode Island has an interest in and an obligation to secure the safety and wellbeing of its residents, to protect its authority under our national and state constitutions and state laws, and to promote the rule of law as outlined in those documents. Such a task force enables the state to formally gather information and proactively make recommendations where it has the authority to do so.”

Representative David Morales (Democrat, District 7, Providence) introduced House Bill 8139 to ensure that “the state would cover dialysis for the treatment of end-stage renal disease and kidney transplants for persons who do not qualify for full Medicaid due to their immigration status.” Representative Morales explained:

“This legislation allows for our undocumented neighbors and non-citizens who would otherwise meet the income eligibility requirements for Medicaid, but are excluded simply because of their status. Without regular dialysis, kidney failure becomes fatal. Dialysis essentially performs the life-sustaining function of filtering toxins from the blood when the kidneys can no longer do so. Unfortunately, because we do not offer this service through the means of Medicaid, we have many of our neighbors who are forced to wait until their condition becomes life-threatening. Then they arrive at the emergency room at the brink of death.”

Representative David Place (Republican, District 47, Burrillville, Glocester) worried that the bill would make Rhode Island a “destination for this particular type of care.” Representative Place explained that “my understanding of the five-year residency requirement is to show that you are committed to living here. You’re not showing up here to get health coverage, but you’re actually living here. My concern is that we don’t become a destination for this particular type of care … because it’ll become very cost-prohibitive, very fast.”

“My concern,” countered Representative Morales, “is about the countless neighbors who have found themselves in this predicament, where they cannot receive the service and care that they need. And regardless, as taxpayers, we end up footing the bill when it comes through emergency care.”

Representative Jenni Furtado (Democrat, District 64, East Providence, Pawtucket) introduced House Bill 8140, seeking to bar state and local police from joining federal immigration enforcement programs, limit holding people on detainers without judicial warrants, and require the tracking and reporting of requests from federal immigration authorities.

Representative Enrique Sanchez (Democrat, District 9, Providence) introduced House Resolution 7131, extending the House’s deepest condolences to the family of Renee Good, the innocent mother of three murdered in Minneapolis by an ICE agent.

name: Policy
title: Trump fires Homeland Security Secretary Noem after building criticism over immigration enforcement
description:
WASHINGTON (AP) — President Donald Trump on Thursday fired his embattled Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem and said he will nominate in her place Oklahoma Republican Sen. Markwayne Mullin.
Trump made the announcement on social media on Thursday, two days after Noem faced a grilling on Capitol Hill from GOP members as well as Democrats.
Noem has faced waves of criticism as she’s overseen Trump’s immigration crackdown, especially since the shooting deaths of two protesters in Minneapolis at the hands of immigration enforcement officers.
Aside from immigration, Noem also faced criticism — including from Republicans — over the pace of emergency funding approved through the Federal Emergency Management Agency and for the Trump administration’s response to disasters.
Mullin would need to be confirmed by the Senate, but under a federal law governing executive branch vacancies, he would be allowed to serve as an acting Homeland Security secretary as long as his nomination is formally pending.
text:
WASHINGTON (AP) — President Donald Trump on Thursday fired his embattled Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem and said he will nominate in her place Oklahoma Republican Sen. Markwayne Mullin.

Trump made the announcement on social media on Thursday, two days after Noem faced a grilling on Capitol Hill from GOP members as well as Democrats.

Trump says he’ll make Noem a “Special Envoy for The Shield of the Americas,” a new security initiative that he said would focus on the Western Hemisphere.

Noem is the first Cabinet secretary to leave during Trump’s second term. Noem’s departure caps a tumultuous tenure overseeing immigration enforcement tactics that have been met with protests and lawsuits.

Noem has faced waves of criticism as she’s overseen Trump’s immigration crackdown, especially since the shooting deaths of two protesters in Minneapolis at the hands of immigration enforcement officers. The former South Dakota governor was also criticized over the way her department has spent billions of dollars allocated to it by Congress.

Frustrations over Noem’s execution of the Republican president’s hard-line immigration agenda — particularly her leadership after the shooting deaths of the two U.S. citizens in Minneapolis — as well as her handling of disaster response, paved the way for her downfall. She faced blistering criticism from Democrats, and some Republicans, in Congress hearings this week over those issues and others.

Aside from immigration, Noem also faced criticism — including from Republicans — over the pace of emergency funding approved through the Federal Emergency Management Agency and for the Trump administration’s response to disasters.

Mullin would need to be confirmed by the Senate, but under a federal law governing executive branch vacancies, he would be allowed to serve as an acting Homeland Security secretary as long as his nomination is formally pending.

name: Policy
title: Noem out, Trump picks Mullin as DHS secretary - POLITICO
authors: Jordain Carney, Myah Ward, Eric Bazail-Eimil
description:
“We look forward to working closer with the White House.
Those discussions involved people at the highest levels of the Trump administration, including chief of staff Susie Wiles, according to four of the people.
“POTUS did not sign off on a $220 million dollar ad campaign,” said a White House official.
A person familiar with the conversations around removing Noem said Trump and Noem spoke before he announced her job change on social media.
The GOP criticism of Noem intensified earlier this year, with White House allies blaming her for the chaotic fallout from the DHS enforcement surge in Minneapolis.
text:
Trump’s post went out about 20 minutes before Noem began speaking at the Sergeant Benevolent Association Major Cities Conference in Tennessee. Noem did not mention her change of title during prepared remarks. She did address her new role dealing with Western Hemisphere nations in a social media post later Thursday, saying, “I look forward to working with them closely to dismantle cartels that have poured drugs into our nation and killed our children and grandchildren.” She also hailed “historic accomplishments” under her leadership at DHS.

Trump’s message indicated he intends to nominate Mullin to the Cabinet. It appeared the president wants him in that role by March 31, but it‘s unclear if the Senate would act that quickly to confirm him.

In a separate post, White House spokesperson Karoline Leavitt clarified that the administration “will work with the Senate to confirm the extraordinarily qualified Senator @MarkwayneMullin as the next Secretary of the Department of Homeland Security as quickly as possible.”

“The president and I are good friends,” Mullin told reporters shortly after the announcement. “We look forward to working closer with the White House. Obviously, I‘m gonna be over there a lot more.”

Senior administration officials in the last few days had privately urged Trump to replace Noem, according to three Republicans with direct knowledge of the effort and a person close to the White House granted anonymity to disclose the private push.

The secretary faced intense scrutiny and calls from some GOP lawmakers to resign over her management of the sprawling department — including her response to federal agents killing two U.S. citizens in Minneapolis in January.

But the frustration inside the administration — brewing for months — stretched well beyond that, including concerns over how she used some DHS funding and her relationship with her special adviser Corey Lewandowski, who has played an outsize role at the agency.

This article is based on conversations with 10 people, including administration officials, former Trump officials, people close to the White House and Hill Republicans, who were granted anonymity to discuss private conversations.

Those discussions involved people at the highest levels of the Trump administration, including chief of staff Susie Wiles, according to four of the people. Border czar Tom Homan, who has often clashed with the DHS secretary, has been a key driver lobbying the president to remove her, said one of the people, who is close to the White House.

Frustration grew more serious in recent days. Noem’s testimony before the Senate Judiciary Committee on Tuesday sparked angst within the White House including with Trump, according to two people with knowledge of the president’s private conversations.

During the hearing, Noem was asked about spending hundreds of millions of dollars on ads in which she featured prominently. She told senators the president approved of the controversial blitz. The ads, according to two of the people, are a sore spot within the White House, including with Trump, because they feed into a perception that Noem is using her position to set herself up for a future political run.

“POTUS did not sign off on a $220 million dollar ad campaign,” said a White House official. “Absolutely not.”

Trump in conversations this week had gone so far as to quiz people he is talking with on potential replacements for Noem, according to two of the people, who both said Trump would fire the secretary. He floated Mullin, a close ally.

A person familiar with the conversations around removing Noem said Trump and Noem spoke before he announced her job change on social media. Lewandowski is expected to leave DHS too, according to the person.

Mullin said he found out about his nomination before Trump’s post went out.

The GOP criticism of Noem intensified earlier this year, with White House allies blaming her for the chaotic fallout from the DHS enforcement surge in Minneapolis. Republicans criticized her for rushing to speak on television in the wake of the killings of Renee Good and Alex Pretti by federal agents, arguing that her approach undermined public trust in ongoing investigations.

The criticisms of DHS leadership also apply to Lewandowski, as current and former administration officials have questioned how he’s counting his days at the agency — given he was originally tapped as a special governmental employee, a temporary role that is supposed to be limited to 130 days per year of unpaid work.

A DHS spokesperson did not immediately respond to a request for comment on Lewandowski’s future.

Noem this week also refused to answer questions from House Democrats about her relationship with her special adviser, including whether they had “sexual relations” during her tenure at DHS.

Other outlets have alleged the two have had an intimate relationship, though both denied the claims.

Lewandowski has also been involved in greenlighting any contract exceeding $100,000 at the agency, as well as other high-level decisions. An administration official, granted anonymity to speak candidly, said that Lewandowski is also in trouble, pointing to DHS’s aborted decision last month to shutdown TSA PreCheck as a bargaining maneuver aimed at forcing Democrats to fund the department, among other incidents.

“All the contracts and other scandals are facing recurring scrutiny,” said the administration official. “No one is talking about the most secure border ever. Just the shitshow that is Noem and Corey.”

The DHS spokesperson, when asked about Lewandowski’s role in the temporary shutdown of TSA PreCheck, said the program “remains operational with no change for the traveling public” and that “TSA will evaluate on a case by case basis at the local airport level and adjust operations accordingly” as staffing constraints arise.

Noem, formerly a House member and then the governor of South Dakota, had little background on national security issues when Trump tapped her to take the reins of DHS. But she earned the position after lobbying from Lewandowski, jumping into a sprawling agency that manages immigration enforcement, counterterrorism, intelligence collection, disaster relief and the nation’s cybersecurity defenses.

She was quickly dogged by scandals around her spending of funds allocated to the department for immigration enforcement under the One Big Beautiful Bill. Noem was pilloried by Democrats for spending millions of dollars to buy what they characterized as “luxury” jets that DHS claimed would be used for deportations and “command and control” flights. Democrats also alleged that Noem personally benefited from her service as Homeland Security secretary after reports emerged she was living rent free in the government-owned house typically reserved for the Coast Guard commandant.

DHS’s Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency experienced major staff cuts under her watch, at a time when the U.S. faces pronounced cyberthreats from foreign adversaries and criminal hackers.

Detractors have also argued her overhaul of the Federal Emergency Management Agency has done little to address inefficiencies at the agency, while making it more difficult for the federal government to respond to crises. Similar disruptions to DHS’ intelligence arm have raised questions among law enforcement groups about whether DHS is still equipped to respond to terror attacks.

Republicans are uniformly largely praising Trump’s selection of Mullin as a good move for the department. While most Republicans are taking pains to still praise Noem, some of her detractors took glee in her removal from the post.

Sen. Thom Tillis (R-N.C.) called Mullin “a great choice to lead DHS, restore competence, and refocus efforts” at the department and noted Mullin “likes dogs,” a dig at Noem in light of a story she included in her memoir about shooting a young dog she was training.

Mullin joined the Senate from the House in 2023, making him still a relative newcomer by the chamber’s standards. But his close ties to House Republicans and Trump helped him quickly rise within the conference, including serving as an informal adviser to Senate Majority Leader John Thune, who informally referred to him as their “whisperer.”

Over the past year, Mullin has played key roles in trying to help navigate between the Senate, House and the White House. That includes acting as a go-between when the House and Senate were scuffling over the fate of the One Big Beautiful Bill, including helping organize a meeting between Thune and House Ways and Means Chair Jason Smith (R-Mo.), who is a close friend of the Oklahoma Republican.

Mullin was also a key conduit for the White House and Senate Republicans during last year’s government funding fight and a failed deal last summer to try to negotiate an agreement to quickly confirm a tranche of nominees.

“Fresh set of eyes. Fresh approach. And you know, obviously, the one thing that everybody has said on handling some of the immigration policy, ICE issues, is to be strategic about it, prioritize,” Thune said. “I think some of the things that Tom Homan has emphasized since he’s been more heavily engaged on the scene. … I think he and Senator Mullin can work well together. ”
